title: Tennessee State vs Iowa State — First Round Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • First Round • St. Louis, MO
venue: Enterprise Center, St. Louis, Missouri • TBS
extraInfo: Winner faces #7 Kentucky or #10 Santa Clara on Sunday

stakes.callout: An HBCU making its first NCAA Tournament appearance in 32 years, coached by a Duke national champion and former NBA first-round pick, against a 2-seed featuring a player having one of the greatest three-point shooting seasons in college basketball history. Tennessee State's OVC Player of the Year transferred from Division III. Their sharpshooter was recruited twice before going to D-II. Their point guard has played at four different schools. This is a team of journeymen who found each other in Nashville. Iowa State started 16-0 and has the 6th-best defense in America.

cards.label: For Tennessee State
cards.value: First NCAA Tournament in 32 years. First OVC championship since 1994. An HBCU on the national stage — the school that produced Oprah Winfrey and Wilma Rudolph. Nolan Smith won a national championship at Duke as a player; now he's led Tennessee State to March Madness in his first year as a head coach.

cards.label: For Iowa State
cards.value: Started 16-0 (best in program history) and reached #2 in the AP poll (also a program high). Milan Momcilovic is shooting 49.6% from three on 127 makes — arguably the best three-point season in college basketball history. Iowa State has never made a Final Four. This is their best path.

cards.label: The D-III to OVC MVP Pipeline
cards.value: Aaron Nkrumah transferred from Worcester State (Division III) to Tennessee State and became the OVC Player of the Year. Travis Harper went to Clayton State (Division II) before making the jump. Dante Harris has played at four schools. These are not typical 15-seed players — they're survivors.

cards.label: The HBCU Factor
cards.value: Tennessee State is Tennessee's only state-funded HBCU. Their Tigerbelles track program produced 40 Olympic athletes. This tournament appearance is bigger than basketball — it's visibility for an entire institution and community that hasn't had this national spotlight in three decades.

styles.title: HBCU Defense and Journeymen Guards
bullets: Elite defense for a 15-seed: Tennessee State ranks 56th nationally in KenPom defensive efficiency (101.5). That's not just good for a 15-seed — it's genuinely good. They allow 73.3 PPG and force turnovers at an elite rate. Aaron Nkrumah's 5.0% steal rate ranks 6th nationally.

Three-headed guard attack: Nkrumah (17.6 PPG), Harper (17.3), and Harris (11.7, 4.5 APG) give Tennessee State three legitimate scoring options. Harper shoots 40.1% from three. Nkrumah creates havoc with steals. Harris has played at Georgetown, Virginia, Memphis, and now TSU — he's seen it all.

OVC Tournament dominance: Tennessee State won the OVC championship 93-67, shooting 61.5% from three. They didn't just win — they destroyed Morehead State. That kind of shooting confidence is dangerous in a single-elimination tournament.

Nolan Smith's Duke DNA: Smith won a national championship at Duke and was an All-American. He learned from Coach K. His first year at TSU: OVC champions, NCAA Tournament, Co-Coach of the Year. The preparation and discipline show.

Weakness: Offensive efficiency ranks 136th nationally. Iowa State's defense is 6th. The math is brutal. TSU also has limited size — nobody taller than 6-9 in the regular rotation against Iowa State's Blake Buchanan (6-11). Free throw shooting could be a liability.

styles.title: Historic Shooting and Elite Defense
bullets: Momcilovic is having a historic season: The 6-8 junior is shooting 49.6% from three on 127 makes — both Iowa State single-season records. He's the first power-conference player at 6-8+ to shoot over 50% from three in college basketball history. He's made eight threes in a game four different times. Julius Erving Award finalist.

Defense wins in March: Iowa State ranks 6th nationally in KenPom defensive efficiency (95.6). Tamin Lipsey is one of the best on-ball defenders in the Big 12. They contest everything, force turnovers, and don't give up easy baskets. This defense has been the foundation of a 27-7 season.

Jefferson does everything: Joshua Jefferson (16.9 PPG, 7.6 RPG, 4.9 APG) is an AP All-America Second Team selection. A 6-8 forward who scores, rebounds, passes, and defends. He transferred from Saint Mary's and has been the Cyclones' most versatile player.

Started 16-0: Iowa State opened with 16 straight wins — the best start in program history. They reached #2 in the AP poll, also a program best. Even after some Big 12 turbulence (12-6), they remained a 2-seed.

Weakness: Free throw shooting is 67.2% — genuinely bad for a 2-seed. If this game gets close and Iowa State is at the line, TSU has a chance. Also lost to Arizona in the Big 12 Tournament semis, showing vulnerability when pressured.

keys.title: Can TSU's defense slow Iowa State's offense?
keys.description: Tennessee State's defense ranks 56th in KenPom. Iowa State's offense ranks 21st. This is a matchup of TSU's best attribute against ISU's second-best. If TSU can force turnovers and contest Momcilovic's threes, they can keep this competitive. If Iowa State gets clean looks from three, the shooting differential is too great.

keys.title: Momcilovic vs. TSU's perimeter defense.
keys.description: Milan Momcilovic is shooting 49.6% from three — the best in the country. TSU's guards are aggressive on-ball defenders led by Nkrumah's 87 steals. The question: can TSU's defensive pressure bother a shooter this accurate, or will their aggression create open looks for Momcilovic when they over-commit? This is the key matchup.

keys.title: Iowa State's free throw problem.
keys.description: Iowa State shoots 67.2% from the free throw line. That's terrible for a 2-seed. If TSU can keep this game in the 60s or low 70s and force late-game free throws, Iowa State's Achilles' heel could come into play. Lipsey (64.4% FT) and Buchanan (47.8% FT) are particular concerns.

keys.title: The size mismatch.
keys.description: Iowa State's Blake Buchanan is 6-11 and shoots 64.5% from the field. TSU's tallest regular rotation player is Antoine Lorick III at 6-9. Jefferson at 6-8 with 7.6 RPG will dominate the glass. If Iowa State pounds the ball inside, TSU has no answer. TSU needs to turn this into a perimeter game.

keys.title: The 32-year wait.
keys.description: Tennessee State hasn't been in the tournament since 1994. Some of these players weren't alive then. The weight of that history could inspire or overwhelm. Nolan Smith's championship pedigree — his calm, his experience, his Duke DNA — is the antidote to tournament anxiety. If TSU plays loose and aggressive, their defensive identity could create problems. If they play tight, it's over fast.

prediction.summary: The talent gap is real, but Tennessee State's defensive identity gives them a puncher's chance of keeping this competitive. TSU ranks 56th in defensive efficiency — that's no joke — and their trio of Nkrumah, Harper, and Harris have all played through adversity that makes tournament pressure feel familiar. The problem? Iowa State's offense ranks 21st, Momcilovic is shooting nearly 50% from three, and Jefferson does everything at 16.9/7.6/4.9. Iowa State should pull away in the second half, but don't be surprised if TSU is within single digits at halftime.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Tennessee State comes out with energy and emotion — 32 years of waiting will do that. Nkrumah's defensive pressure creates a couple of early turnovers, and Harper hits an early three. But Momcilovic answers from deep, and Jefferson's all-around game keeps Iowa State composed. It's a tight 15-12 type start.

phases.title: Mid-First Half
phases.description: Iowa State's defense (6th nationally) starts to clamp down on TSU's guards. Harris, despite his four-school experience, struggles against Lipsey's elite on-ball defense. Buchanan's 6-11 frame creates problems inside that Lorick can't solve. Iowa State pushes the lead to 8-10 by halftime.

phases.title: Early Second Half
phases.description: TSU makes a run. Nolan Smith's Duke DNA shows in halftime adjustments — he switches to more ball-screen action to free Harper, who starts hitting mid-range pull-ups. Nkrumah gets a couple of steals that turn into transition baskets. The lead shrinks to 5. The crowd gets into it.

phases.title: Final 10 Minutes
phases.description: This is where Iowa State's talent separates. Momcilovic hits a dagger three from the wing. Jefferson controls the glass. TSU's lack of size becomes fatal as Buchanan converts easy looks inside. Iowa State's free throw shooting (67.2%) keeps the door slightly ajar, but not enough for TSU to walk through it.

projectedScore.rationale: Iowa State's size, Momcilovic's shooting, and the 6th-ranked defense are too much for TSU. Tennessee State keeps it respectable behind Nkrumah and Harper, but the talent gap shows in the second half.

players.name: Aaron Nkrumah
players.meta: F • 6-6 • Senior • OVC Player of the Year
players.bio:
From Worcester, Massachusetts. Transferred from Worcester State — a Division III school. Made All-OVC Second Team last year, then became the OVC Player of the Year this season. Ranks 6th nationally in steal rate (5.0%). Scored 30 against Morehead State. Only the third Tennessee State player to win OVC POY, joining Carlos Rogers (1993-94). A D-III to OVC MVP story you couldn't make up.
Read more →

players.name: Travis Harper II
players.bio:
From Columbus, Georgia. Tennessee State recruited him twice out of high school, but COVID disrupted his path. He chose Clayton State (Division II) instead. "I took a chance on myself," he said. Now he's shooting 40.1% from three and averaging 17.3 PPG in the NCAA Tournament. TSU came back for him — and he delivered.
Read more →

players.name: Dante Harris
players.meta: G • 6-0 • 6th-Year Senior • Georgetown → Virginia → Memphis → TSU
players.bio:
A sixth-year senior who has played at four schools: Georgetown, Virginia, Memphis, and Tennessee State. Called "the winner Tennessee State needed." His experience running point guard at Power Five programs gives TSU a steadiness that most 15-seeds lack. This is his last dance — and he's playing for a program that's been waiting 32 years.
Read more →

players.name: Antoine Lorick III
players.meta: F • 6-9 • Junior • OVC Tournament Most Outstanding Player
players.bio: Junior forward who earned OVC Tournament Most Outstanding Player honors after a double-double in the semifinal. At 6-9, he's TSU's tallest starter and biggest hope for contesting Iowa State's interior game. Shoots 52.0% from the field.

players.name: Milan Momcilovic
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Junior • Julius Erving Award Finalist
players.bio:
From Menomonee Falls, Wisconsin. Has made 127 three-pointers at 49.6% — both Iowa State single-season records. The first power-conference player at 6-8+ to shoot over 50% from three in college basketball history. Made eight threes in a game four times. His 2025-26 season is being called one of the greatest three-point campaigns ever.
Read more →

players.name: Joshua Jefferson
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Senior • AP All-America Second Team
players.bio:
From Henderson, Nevada (Liberty HS — won a state championship). Transferred from Saint Mary's after two seasons. AP All-America Second Team. Averages 16.9/7.6/4.9 — a rare stat line that shows he does everything. The heart and soul of this Cyclones team in his final college season.
Read more →

players.name: Tamin Lipsey
players.bio: The rare four-year player in the transfer portal era. Has been with Iowa State since freshman year and has grown into one of the best point guards in the Big 12. Leads the team with 5.0 APG and is an elite on-ball defender who sets the tone for the 6th-ranked defense in America.

players.name: Blake Buchanan
players.bio: Transfer from Boise State who provides Iowa State with interior presence at 6-11. Shoots 64.5% from the field — an elite finisher around the rim. Creates a massive size mismatch against Tennessee State's frontcourt. The caveat: shoots just 47.8% from the free throw line.

funFacts.title: Oprah Went Here (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: Oprah Winfrey graduated from Tennessee State University in 1987. Other famous alumni include Olympic legends Wilma Rudolph (3 golds, 1960) and Ralph Boston, plus NFL Hall of Famer Richard Dent (Super Bowl XX MVP).

funFacts.title: Duke Champion to HBCU Coach
funFacts.body: Nolan Smith won a national championship at Duke in 2010, was the ACC Player of the Year in 2011, and was drafted 21st overall by the Trail Blazers. Now he's coaching at an HBCU — and won the OVC in his first year. His father Derek played in the NBA and died of cardiac arrest at age 34.

funFacts.title: The Greatest Shooting Season? (Iowa State)
funFacts.body: Milan Momcilovic has made 127 three-pointers at 49.6%. He's the first power-conference player at 6-8 to shoot over 50% from three. He's made eight threes in a single game four times. His season is being called one of the greatest three-point campaigns in college basketball history.

funFacts.title: Division III to OVC MVP (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: Aaron Nkrumah transferred from Worcester State (Division III) to Tennessee State and became the OVC Player of the Year. He ranks 6th nationally in steal rate. The D-III to conference MVP pipeline is not a thing — except at TSU.

funFacts.title: Hilton Magic (Iowa State)
funFacts.body: Iowa State's home court, Hilton Coliseum, is famous for "Hilton Magic" — the arena's legendary reputation for hosting dramatic upsets and unforgettable moments. The student section "Cyclone Alley" creates one of the loudest environments in college basketball.

funFacts.title: 40 Olympic Athletes (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: TSU's Tigerbelles women's track program produced 40 Olympic athletes, including Wilma Rudolph and Wyomia Tyus. It's one of the most decorated athletic programs in American history — from a school with 7,600 students.

funFacts.title: "I Took a Chance on Myself" (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: Travis Harper II was recruited by TSU twice out of high school, but COVID derailed his path. He went to Division II Clayton State instead. Eventually TSU came back for him. Now he's shooting 40.1% from three in the NCAA Tournament.

funFacts.title: 16-0 Start (Iowa State)
funFacts.body: Iowa State opened the season 16-0 — the best start in program history. They also reached #2 in the AP poll, the highest ranking in program history. The Cyclones' name dates to 1895, when a reporter said they "swept over" Northwestern "like a cyclone."

funFacts.title: Four Schools, One Final Dance (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: Dante Harris is a sixth-year senior who has played at Georgetown, Virginia, Memphis, and Tennessee State. He was called "the winner Tennessee State needed." This is his last season of college basketball — at a program's first tournament in 32 years.

funFacts.title: Tennessee's Only State-Funded HBCU
funFacts.body: Tennessee State was founded in 1912 and is the only state-funded historically Black university in Tennessee. The university's motto: "Think. Work. Serve." The 2026 tournament appearance puts the entire institution on the national stage.

funFacts.title: No Final Four... Yet (Iowa State)
funFacts.body: Iowa State hasn't made a Final Four since 1944. They reached the Elite Eight in 2000 and the Sweet 16 in 2024. A 2-seed gives them their best path in years. Momcilovic, Jefferson, and Lipsey are the core trying to make history.

funFacts.title: 32 Years Between Dances (Tennessee State)
funFacts.body: Tennessee State last appeared in the NCAA Tournament in 1994, when they lost to Kentucky as a 14-seed. The 32-year gap is one of the longest active droughts broken this year. Some current players weren't born when TSU last danced.

history.callout: Iowa State has been to 25 NCAA Tournaments but has never reached a Final Four. Tennessee State is making its first tournament appearance in 32 years — and only its third in the Division I era. The HBCU that produced Oprah Winfrey and Wilma Rudolph is on the national stage for the first time since 1994, led by a Duke national champion coaching his first season.

timeline.text: Tennessee State makes the NCAA Tournament for the first time in the Division I era. Lost to Seton Hall.
TSU returns as a 14-seed. Lost to Kentucky, 83-70. The program would not return to March Madness for 32 years.
Nolan Smith — Duke national champion, All-American, former NBA player — hired as head coach in July. Takes over a 17-16 team.
23-9. OVC regular season co-champions. OVC Tournament champions with a 93-67 blowout of Morehead State (61.5% from three). First NCAA Tournament in 32 years. The drought is over.

subsections.title: Iowa State's Quest for a Final Four
subsections.content:
Iowa State has been a tournament regular under T.J. Otzelberger — Sweet 16 in 2024, NCAA Tournament in 2025, and now a 2-seed in 2026. But the Cyclones have never reached a Final Four in program history. Their best runs: Elite Eight in 2000 under Larry Eustachy and Sweet 16 in 2024.

This year's team started 16-0, reached #2 in the AP poll, and features Milan Momcilovic's historically great three-point shooting (49.6%, 127 makes). Joshua Jefferson is an AP All-American. Tamin Lipsey has been the steady hand for four years. The pieces are in place for a deep run — but first, they need to handle an HBCU team with the 56th-ranked defense in America and nothing to lose.

subsections.title: The HBCU in March
subsections.content:
HBCUs in the NCAA Tournament have a complicated history — full of pride, limited resources, and occasional magic. Tennessee State joins a tradition that includes Norfolk State's upset of Missouri in 2012 and Fairleigh Dickinson's upset of Purdue in 2023 (FDU is not an HBCU, but the principle holds for small schools).

What makes TSU's appearance special goes beyond basketball. This is a school with 7,600 students and a budget that can't compete with Iowa State's. But they have Nolan Smith's Duke pedigree, Aaron Nkrumah's D-III-to-OVC-MVP story, and 32 years of built-up desire. Win or lose, this game puts Tennessee State — and its legacy of Oprah, Wilma Rudolph, and 40 Olympic athletes — on the biggest stage in college sports.
